title: Balsamic vinegar
desc: A sweet-sour condiment built from cooked grape must, not fermented wine. Ranges from cheap caramel-colored supermarket glaze to decades-aged Modena DOP. Reduces to a syrup that balances acid against caramelized fruit sugar.

title: Brussels sprouts
desc: Brassica oleracea var. gemmifera. Lateral-bud cabbages whose glucosinolate sulfur turns sweet and nutty under dry high heat and acrid when boiled. Bitterness has been bred down dramatically since the 1990s.

title: Balsamic glaze
desc: A mid-grade IGP balsamic vinegar simmered down to roughly a third of its volume, optionally rounded with a little honey, until it coats a spoon and sets to a glossy, clinging syrup on cooling. The reduction concentrates both the cooked-grape-must sweetness and the acetic acid, so the glaze stays sweet-sour and sharp rather than one-note sweet. The drizzle behind roasted vegetables, seared meats, ripe fruit, and good cheese.

title: Crispy fried brussels sprouts with fish sauce caramel
desc: Halved brussels sprouts deep-fried until the leaves blister crisp and the cut faces go deep brown, then tossed in a Vietnamese-style caramel of sugar cooked to deep amber and let down with fish sauce, lime, garlic, and chili. The fry crisps the brassica leaves into shatter-light chips, and the salty-sweet-sour-funky sauce plays directly against the sulfur and char. This is the restaurant dish that converted a generation to brussels sprouts.

ingredients.item: neutral oil for deep-frying (rice bran, peanut, or canola)

title: Fish sauce
desc: Whole small fish, usually anchovies, layered with sea salt at roughly a 3-to-1 ratio and left to ferment and autolyze for six months to a year, until the fish dissolve into a clear amber liquid driven by their own enzymes and salt-tolerant bacteria. The first-press liquid drawn off the top is the prized nuoc mam nhi or nam pla; what remains can be re-extracted with brine for a second-grade sauce. The salt of an entire region of cooking, built from two ingredients and time.

ingredients.item: fresh whole anchovies (or sardines, smelt, or other small oily fish)
coarse sea salt (non-iodized, no anti-caking agents)

title: Roasted brussels sprouts with balsamic and parmesan
desc: Halved sprouts roasted cut-face down at 230 °C until the flat side builds a deep mahogany Maillard crust and the loose outer leaves char to chips, then tossed off the heat with a reduced balsamic glaze and a shower of microplaned parmigiano. The dish is a single sweet-sour-bitter-umami chord: caramelized brassica char, the cooked-fruit sweetness and acid of reduced balsamic, and the glutamate-and-salt of aged hard cheese.

title: Seared steak with charred brussels sprouts and brown butter
desc: A one-pan dinner: brussels sprouts hard-charred cut-face down in a smoking cast-iron skillet, pushed aside while a dry-brined steak sears in the same pan, then both finished in a foaming brown butter spiked with garlic and lemon. The brassica char and mustardy bitterness cut the rendered beef fat the way a mustard or red-wine pan sauce would, and the nutty Maillard milk solids of the brown butter tie the steak and the sprouts into one plate.

ingredients.item: steaks (chuck eye, ribeye, or strip), 1 inch / 2.5 cm thick

title: Shaved brussels sprout salad with pecorino and hazelnut
desc: Raw brussels sprouts shaved paper-thin on a mandoline, dressed with a sharp lemon-dijon vinaigrette that lightly tenderizes the shreds, and tossed with microplaned pecorino, toasted hazelnuts, and shaved raw shallot. Raw sprouts are crisp and faintly peppery rather than sulfurous, hold a dressing for hours without wilting, and read as a bright, dense autumn-winter slaw with the salt of the cheese and the toasted fat of the nuts.

title: Sheet-pan mustard chicken thighs with brussels sprouts
desc: Bone-in, skin-on chicken thighs rubbed with a dijon-maple-thyme paste and roasted on a single sheet pan above halved brussels sprouts, so the rendering chicken fat bastes the sprouts from above while the dry oven crisps the skin. The mustard glaze gives the skin a lacquered tang, the sprouts roast in schmaltz and caramelize against the maple, and a finishing splash of vinegar ties the fat, the sweetness, and the brassica char together.

title: Deep-frying
desc: Cook food submerged in hot oil (typically 160 to 190 °C) so that heat transfers from oil to surface fast enough to dehydrate and crisp the exterior while steam escaping from inside cooks the interior. The crust forms because surface water boils off and the dry surface climbs into the Maillard and starch-crisping range; oil temperature and dryness are the two levers that decide crisp versus greasy.

title: Dry caramel (sugar cooked to amber)
desc: Melt sugar over heat with no added water until it liquefies, then browns through a spectrum of color and flavor toward deep amber. Caramelization is the thermal decomposition of sugar, distinct from the Maillard reaction (which needs amino acids); the browning is sucrose breaking into hundreds of new aromatic compounds. The depth of color is the depth of flavor, and the window before bitter-burnt is narrow.

title: Gochujang-honey chicken tenders with smashed cucumber salad
desc: Chicken tenderloins quick-brined in soy, ginger, and garlic, then dusted in cornstarch for a thin Maillard crust and seared in a smoking carbon-steel pan. Off-heat, the same pan builds a glaze of gochujang, honey, rice vinegar, and toasted sesame oil that lacquers the tenders in 30 seconds. Served with smashed Persian cucumbers salt-drained then tossed in rice vinegar, gochugaru, garlic, and sesame, the cool acid-and-crunch foil to the warm sweet-spicy glaze. Lean, fast (under 30 minutes), and structurally healthier than fried tenders: no breading, no deep-fry, the carbs come from a single tablespoon of honey rather than a sugar-loaded bottled sauce.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (high smoke point: avocado, rice bran, or grapeseed)

title: Lemongrass chicken tenders with Vietnamese rice noodle bowl
desc: Chicken tenderloins marinated 30 minutes in a paste of pounded lemongrass, garlic, shallot, fish sauce, palm sugar, and black pepper, then hard-seared in a smoking carbon-steel pan until the sugar lacquers into a mahogany crust. Served bun-thit-nuong style over room-temperature rice vermicelli with a fresh-herb plate (mint, cilantro, Thai basil), shredded romaine, julienned cucumber, quick-pickled carrot, crushed roasted peanuts, and a small bowl of nuoc cham to spoon over at the table. Vietnamese in the structural sense: the protein is one component of five, the herbs are not garnish but a primary ingredient eaten by the handful, and the dressing is built in the bowl bite by bite rather than tossed in advance. Lean, vegetable-forward, and naturally gluten-free.

ingredients.item: palm sugar or light brown sugar

title: Chinese five-spice powder (五香粉)
desc: A ground blend of star anise, fennel, clove, cinnamon, and Sichuan peppercorn, balanced to span the five Chinese flavor categories (sweet, sour, bitter, pungent, salty in the traditional cosmology; anethole-dominant sweet-spice in the cup). The signature spice of southern Chinese red-braising, master stocks, char siu, and crisp-skin roasted duck.

title: Monosodium glutamate (MSG, 味精)
desc: The sodium salt of glutamic acid, refined to a white crystalline powder. The purest delivery vehicle for umami in any pantry: glutamate without the body, color, funk, or salt load that comes with soy sauce, miso, fish sauce, or parmesan.

title: Shanghai noodles (cu mian, 粗面)
desc: Thick, chewy round wheat noodles, roughly 2 to 3 mm in diameter, made from flour, water, and salt with no egg and no alkaline (kansui). The starch-heavy noodle behind Shanghai-style stir-fries (cu chao mian), hearty soups, and the noodle that holds up to rich brown sauces without dissolving.

title: Beef and mushroom noodle soup with xiao long bao aspic
desc: A homestyle improvisation built on a cup of leftover xiao long bao pork-skin aspic that melts into a 4-cup beef broth, doubling the broth body and turning a thin soup into something silken. 1 lb of ground beef hard-seared to fond, ginger and scallion and star anise bloomed in the rendered fat, a half teaspoon of Chinese five-spice for a hint of red-braise warmth, dried shiitake (rehydrated, soaking liquid kept) and a pinch of MSG for stacked glutamate plus guanylate, baby Yukon Gold potatoes simmered into the broth for starch and bite, a cluster of blue oyster mushrooms dry-seared and added at the end for layered umami and meaty pull, finished with thick Shanghai noodles, a drizzle of Chinkiang vinegar and toasted sesame oil, white pepper, and scallion greens. The technique trick is the aspic: a cup of chilled pork-skin gel concentrates more collagen than 4 hours of bone simmer, and it dissolves into the broth in two minutes of warming. A leftover-driven dish that eats like a planned one.

ingredients.item: dried shiitake mushroom (donko grade if available)
neutral oil (refined avocado, grapeseed, or rice bran)
kosher salt (for seasoning the beef during the sear)
fresh ginger, peeled and sliced into 3 mm coins
scallions, white and pale-green parts only (reserve the dark greens for garnish)
xiao long bao aspic (leftover, chilled and firm)
baby Yukon Gold potatoes (or any waxy baby potato)
blue oyster mushroom cluster (Pleurotus ostreatus var. columbinus)
fresh Shanghai noodles (cu mian, thick round wheat noodles)
toasted sesame oil (for finishing each bowl)
Chinkiang black vinegar (for finishing each bowl)
white pepper, freshly ground (for finishing each bowl)
scallion greens, sliced thin (reserved from Phase 3)

title: Ube halaya crinkle cookies
desc: Filipino-American bakery staple: ube halaya stands in for melted chocolate as the violet, sweet, fat-and-starch-bearing base. Cornstarch keeps the crumb tender against the jam moisture, and a double sugar dredge cracks vivid violet against snowy powdered-sugar tops.

title: Ube halaya French toast
desc: Stale brioche soaked in a violet custard of whole milk, heavy cream, eggs, and ube halaya, then pan-fried in butter until the surface caramelizes around a custardy-violet interior. Finished with the canonical Filipino coconut cream and toasted coconut.

ingredients.item: coconut cream, scooped off the top of a refrigerated full-fat coconut milk can

title: Ube halaya no-bake cheesecake bars
desc: Toasted coconut graham crust under a layer of cream cheese, mascarpone, ube halaya, and gelatin-stabilized whipped cream. The gelatin sets the filling firm enough to slice cleanly; halaya provides nearly all the sweetness, so no added sugar in the filling itself.

title: Ube halaya pancakes
desc: Filipino-leaning breakfast stack: ube halaya folded into a buttermilk-soured batter that holds violet through the bake. Coconut cream and toasted desiccated coconut finish the plate in lieu of maple, so the halaya stays the headline flavor.

ingredients.item: coconut cream, scooped off the top of a refrigerated full-fat coconut milk can

title: Cajun cornbread nachos with crawfish-andouille smother
desc: The nacho archetype rebuilt on a Cajun chassis: a thin-baked buttermilk-cornmeal sheet cubed and toasted hard into 2 cm croutons that take the place of tortilla chips, smothered in an étouffée-leaning gravy of andouille, the holy trinity, blond roux, and crawfish tail meat, broiler-bound with melted pepper jack, then finished with a horseradish remoulade crema and a finger-knock of Crystal hot sauce at the table. The fusion bridge is structural rather than gimmick: cornbread is the canonical Cajun starch under étouffée and gumbo, and a properly toasted cornbread cube has the same crisp-shell, absorbent-interior load capacity that a tortilla chip has under chili and cheese.

ingredients.item: bacon fat or lard, for greasing the pan
Cajun seasoning (Tony Chachere's, Slap Ya Mama, or homemade from the blackened-redfish blend)
andouille sausage, sliced 8 mm thick into half-moons
crawfish tail meat with fat (Louisiana, frozen and thawed)
pepper jack cheese, grated from the block on large holes
Dijon mustard (or Creole mustard if you can find Zatarain's)
scallions, thinly sliced on the bias
flat-leaf parsley, leaves picked and roughly chopped
Crystal or Tabasco hot sauce, at the table

title: Lotus root nachos with Sichuan mala pork and silken-tofu queso
desc: The nacho archetype rebuilt on a Sichuan chassis: lotus root sliced 1.5 mm on a mandoline and two-stage deep-fried into glassy wagon-wheel chips that take the place of tortilla chips, draped with a brilliant red mala pork mince (ground pork rendered into doubanjiang oil, finished with Shaoxing, soy, ground Sichuan peppercorn, and a splash of Chinkiang black vinegar), drizzled with a silken-tofu queso (silken tofu blended with white miso, Chinese sesame paste, and light soy, then briefly heated to nappe), scattered with Chinkiang-pickled red onion, scallion, cilantro, sesame, and finished with a pour of Sichuan chili oil (with sediment). The fusion bridge is structural rather than gimmick: lotus chips are the canonical Sichuan dry-pot crunch, the queso replaces dairy with the equivalent umami-fat-creaminess of fermented soy and ground sesame, and the mala pork is essentially Sichuan ground-pork sauce (zha jiang style) reformatted to drape rather than cling to noodles.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (rice bran, peanut, or canola) for deep-frying
Chinese sesame paste (zhima jiang, toasted-sesame, not raw tahini)
red onion, sliced paper-thin (1 mm) on a mandoline
scallion greens, sliced thin on the bias

title: Pantry match
desc: List the ingredients you have. See which recipes are within reach — and which are one or two shopping items away.

title: Ají amarillo paste
desc: The blended paste of the Peruvian yellow chili (Capsicum baccatum). Fruity, moderately hot, the defining flavor of Peruvian cooking.

title: Ají panca paste
desc: The dried-and-blended paste of the deep-red Peruvian chili (Capsicum baccatum). Smoky, mild, raisin-sweet. The marinade base of anticuchos.

title: Allspice (pimento)
desc: The dried unripe berry of Pimenta dioica, a Jamaican evergreen. Tastes like cinnamon, clove, and nutmeg combined into one berry. The defining spice of Jamaican jerk.

title: Almonds
desc: Prunus dulcis. The thinning-and-thickening nut of Mediterranean, Iberian, Middle Eastern, and Mughlai cooking. Used whole, slivered, ground (almond flour), or as the structural emulsifier in almond-based sauces (picada, salsa de almendras).

title: Bagoong
desc: Filipino fermented shrimp or fish paste. Two main forms: bagoong alamang (pink shrimp paste, wet) and bagoong isda (fish paste, brown). Eaten raw with green mango or sauteed.

title: Banana ketchup
desc: Filipino condiment of mashed banana, vinegar, sugar, and spices, tinted red to look like tomato ketchup. World War II improvisation that became a real cuisine staple.

title: Banh trang (Vietnamese rice paper)
desc: Thin, translucent sheets of rice flour and tapioca starch, dried on bamboo mats. The wrapper for goi cuon (fresh rolls) and cha gio (fried rolls). Activated by warm-water dip.

title: Beef brisket
desc: The breast section of the steer, a heavily-worked muscle with significant connective tissue. The canonical cut for long slow braises, smoked barbecue, and pot roasts.

title: Beef heart
desc: The heart muscle of the steer. Lean, dense, mineral-rich; structurally an unusually tender working muscle. The canonical protein for Peruvian anticuchos.

title: Berbere
desc: Ethiopian chili-and-warm-spice blend. Foundational seasoning for every wat (stew) and several dry rubs across Ethiopian and Eritrean cooking.

title: Calamansi
desc: Small Philippine citrus, a lime-mandarin hybrid. Greenish skin, orange flesh, intensely tart with a floral-mandarin top note. The defining acid of Filipino cooking.

title: Candlenut (kemiri)
desc: Waxy, off-white nut from the candlenut tree, related to macadamia. Used pounded into spice pastes as a thickener and fat carrier. Mildly toxic raw, always cooked.

title: Caraway seed
desc: Seed of Carum carvi, a biennial in the parsley family. Anise-leaning warm-bitter aroma from carvone, the defining note of rye bread, sauerkraut, and many Central and Northern European preparations.

title: Dendê oil (red palm oil)
desc: Unrefined red palm oil from the fruit of Elaeis guineensis. Bright orange-red, savory, the structural fat of Bahian Brazilian and West African cooking.

title: Dill (fresh)
desc: Feathery green leaves of Anethum graveolens. Bright, grassy, faintly anise-leaning aroma. A primary herb in Northern, Central, and Eastern European cooking, distinct from a garnish.

title: Dried porcini
desc: Dehydrated Boletus edulis caps. Concentrated umami, woody-earthy aroma, the canonical dried mushroom of Italian, French, and Central European cooking. The soaking liquid is structural.

title: Dry sherry (Fino or Manzanilla)
desc: Fortified wine from Jerez, Spain, aged under flor yeast. Bone-dry, briny, and intensely savory. The structural deglazing liquid of Spanish cooking and an under-recognized umami accelerant.

title: Egusi seeds
desc: Shelled, dried melon seeds (Cucumeropsis or Citrullus species) used ground as the structural thickener in West African egusi soup. Nutty, fatty, mildly bitter, ~50% oil by weight.

title: Galangal
desc: Pale rhizome of Alpinia galanga, related to ginger but flavorally distinct. Piney, citrusy, with a faint medicinal eucalyptus note. Not substitutable with ginger.

title: Green cabbage
desc: Brassica oleracea var. capitata. The tight-headed pale-green cabbage of European and American supermarkets. Structural in slaws, ferments, braises, stuffed-cabbage preparations, and many soups.

title: Green plantain
desc: Unripe Musa paradisiaca, starchy and astringent. The structural starch of West African and Caribbean cooking.

title: Hoisin sauce
desc: Thick, sweet, fermented soybean sauce with garlic, vinegar, chili, and five-spice. The defining condiment of Cantonese roast meats and Southern Vietnamese pho garnish.

title: Holy basil (bai kaprao)
desc: Peppery, clove-forward basil with serrated leaves and green or purple stems. The canonical herb of Thai pad kaprao. Not interchangeable with Thai basil.

title: Hominy (nixtamal)
desc: Whole maize kernels processed with alkali to remove the seed coat and gelatinize the starch. The structural grain of pozole and many Mesoamerican corn dishes.

title: Hungarian paprika
desc: Ground dried sweet pepper from Hungary, the defining spice of Hungarian cooking. Bright, fruity, low-heat to medium-heat depending on grade. Not interchangeable with Spanish pimentón.

title: Jalapeño
desc: Capsicum annuum. Medium-heat green chile pepper, the everyday Mexican and Tex-Mex chile. Used raw in salsas, pickled in escabeche, smoked-and-dried to become chipotle.

title: Kaffir lime leaves (makrut lime leaves)
desc: Glossy, double-lobed leaves of Citrus hystrix. Floral citrus aroma from citronellal, bruised whole in broths or shredded fine in salads. No real substitute.

title: Kecap manis
desc: Indonesian sweet soy sauce. Thick, syrupy, sweetened with palm sugar and aromatized with star anise and galangal. The defining condiment of Javanese cooking.

title: Lard (manteca)
desc: Rendered pork fat. The traditional cooking fat of Mexican, Filipino, Eastern European, and Southern American cooking. Neutral-tasting, high smoke point, structurally tender for pastry.

title: Lemongrass
desc: Tall, fibrous grass stalk with a lemon-pine aroma from citral. The structural aromatic of mainland Southeast Asian cooking; bruised in broths, pounded into spice pastes.

title: Masa harina
desc: Nixtamalized corn flour. The dehydrated, ground form of masa, reconstituted with water for tortillas, tamales, and sopes.

title: Niter kibbeh
desc: Ethiopian spiced clarified butter. The foundational cooking fat under every wat, with ginger, garlic, cardamom, fenugreek, and other aromatics infused into the fat as the milk solids brown and are strained out.

title: Red palm oil
desc: Unrefined oil pressed from the fruit (not kernel) of the African oil palm. Deep red-orange from carotenoids, earthy-vegetal aroma, structural in West African, Brazilian Bahian, and Caribbean cooking.

title: Pigeon peas (gandules)
desc: Small mottled-beige legumes (Cajanus cajan) with a nutty, earthy flavor. The structural bean of Puerto Rican arroz con gandules and many Caribbean rice-and-bean dishes.

title: Poblano pepper
desc: Capsicum annuum. Wide, dark-green, mild-heat chile pepper from Puebla. The canonical chile for chiles rellenos; dried it becomes the ancho. Roast-and-peel before any application.

title: Pork shoulder (Boston butt, picnic)
desc: The upper foreleg of the pig, a heavily-worked muscle with significant intramuscular fat and connective tissue. The canonical braising and slow-cooking cut.

title: Rice vermicelli (bun)
desc: Thin, round rice-flour noodles dried in nests. The cold-noodle base of Vietnamese bun cha and goi cuon. Cooks in 3 minutes, drains, holds at room temperature.

title: Sauerkraut
desc: Lacto-fermented cabbage. Salt-and-cabbage ferment driven by Leuconostoc and Lactobacillus, tangy from lactic acid, structurally important across German, Polish, Alsatian, and Central European cooking.

title: Scotch bonnet
desc: A wrinkled, lantern-shaped, very hot fruity chili (Capsicum chinense) from the Caribbean. The defining heat of Jamaican jerk, Haitian pikliz, and West African pepper sauces.

title: Teff flour
desc: Flour ground from Eragrostis tef, the tiny ancient grain that has been the staple of Ethiopian and Eritrean agriculture for thousands of years. Naturally gluten-free, slightly sweet-malty, the structural flour of injera.

title: Terasi (belacan)
desc: Fermented shrimp paste, sold as a dense purple-grey block. The funk-forward base of Indonesian and Malaysian spice pastes. Must be toasted before use.

title: Thai basil
desc: Anise-forward basil with purple stems and narrow, pointed leaves. The aromatic of Vietnamese pho garnishes and Thai stir-fries. Not interchangeable with Italian basil.

title: Tomatillo
desc: Physalis philadelphica. Small green fruit in a papery husk, structurally a relative of the cape gooseberry rather than the tomato. Tart, vegetal, gel-forming. The canonical green-salsa fruit of central Mexican cooking.

title: Ají de gallina
desc: The Peruvian creamy chicken stew: poached chicken shredded into a thick aromatic sauce of ají amarillo, sweat-soft onion and garlic, evaporated milk, pecan or walnut, parmesan, and a slice of crustless bread soaked in milk as the structural thickener. Served over boiled yellow potatoes with white rice, black olives, and a halved hard-boiled egg.

ingredients.item: chicken breast, bone-in skin-on (or boneless skinless)
crustless white bread (sandwich bread or French bread)
yellow potatoes (waxy varieties: Yukon Gold or Peruvian yellow)

title: Albóndigas en salsa de almendras (Spanish meatballs in almond-saffron sauce)
desc: Pork-and-beef meatballs braised in a sauce thickened with toasted almonds, tomato, saffron, dry sherry, and bread soaked in milk. The structural Andalusian tapas dish: the almonds act as the body of the sauce (replacing the flour or cream a French or Italian cook would use), the sherry deglaze brings the savory backbone, the saffron carries the color and the dish-defining floral aromatic.
equipment: wide heavy skillet or Dutch oven

title: Anticuchos de corazón
desc: The Peruvian beef heart skewer: trimmed cubed heart marinated overnight in ají panca, garlic, vinegar, cumin, and oregano, then skewered and grilled hot and fast over coals until the surface chars and the interior reads tender. The defining street food of Lima and one of the great offal preparations of the world.

ingredients.item: small waxy potatoes (Peruvian yellow or new potatoes)
choclo (Peruvian large-kernel corn) or sweet corn

title: Arroz con pollo Cubano
desc: Cuban chicken-and-rice, the structural one-pot Sunday lunch: bone-in chicken thighs seared, then braised together with rice in a sofrito-and-tomato-stained broth, finished with peas, roasted red pepper, olives, and a glass of beer for body. Comfort food, dinner-party plate, and the dish that defines the Cuban-American kitchen.

ingredients.item: Valencia or bomba rice (or long-grain as fallback)
pale lager beer (Cuban or any pale lager)

title: Avgolemono soup
desc: The Greek chicken-rice-egg-lemon soup: bone-in chicken pieces simmered in water with aromatics to build the broth, the chicken pulled and shredded, short-grain rice cooked into the broth until tender, then finished by tempering a vigorously whisked egg-and-lemon mixture into the hot broth off-heat. Structurally a textbook tempered-egg emulsion: 3 eggs whisked with 100 ml of lemon juice form an acidic foam, which is brought up to broth temperature by drizzling in 250 ml of hot broth while whisking, then poured back into the pot off-heat. The eggs cook gently into a velvety pale yellow sauce that thickens the broth and binds the rice; the lemon brightens the dish to a clean tangy finish. The canonical Greek comfort soup, and the structural foundation for the broader avgolemono sauce family.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken (mixed pieces: 2 thighs, 2 drumsticks, 2 breast halves, or a small whole chicken cut up)
short-grain or medium-grain rice (arborio, carolina, or Greek glasse)
flat-leaf parsley or fresh dill, finely chopped

title: Baba ganoush
desc: The Levantine smoked-eggplant dip: whole eggplants charred directly over a gas flame (or under a broiler, or on a grill) until the skin is fully blackened and the flesh has collapsed to a soft smoky pulp, then drained, mashed with tahini, lemon, garlic, and salt, and crowned with olive oil, pomegranate seeds, mint, and parsley. The structural defining feature is the smoke: oven-roasted eggplant produces a fine dip but a different and inferior one. The fire-char carries the entire identity of the dish, which is why every serious Levantine kitchen owns or improvises a way to char eggplant directly over flame.

ingredients.item: tahini (well-stirred, smooth, single-ingredient Lebanese or Palestinian)

title: Baechu kimchi
desc: Whole-leaf napa cabbage brined to soften the cell walls, then layered with a gochugaru-fish sauce paste and lacto-fermented for 3 to 7 days at room temperature before cold-storing indefinitely.

ingredients.item: coarse non-iodized sea salt or kosher salt
fresh ginger, grated on a microplane

title: Banh mi thit nuong (grilled pork banh mi)
desc: Vietnamese sandwich of grilled lemongrass-marinated pork shoulder on a crisp-shelled, airy-crumb baguette, layered with pickled carrot and daikon (do chua), cucumber, cilantro, jalapeno, a swipe of mayonnaise, and a thread of soy. The structural identity is the contrast between the shattering baguette crust, the soft interior crumb, the smoky-sweet pork, and the bright vinegar-pickled vegetables; each bite needs all four for the sandwich to work.

ingredients.item: light soy sauce, for sandwich finish

title: Beef bourguignon
desc: The Burgundian red-wine beef braise: chuck cubes browned hard, lardon-rendered fat as the structural base, deglazed with a full bottle of dry red Burgundy (or pinot noir), aromatic bouquet garni, slow oven braise 3 hours until the collagen converts to gelatin, finished with sautéed pearl onions and pan-glazed cremini mushrooms tucked in just before service. The structural rule is heat discipline (a tremor, never a boil, throughout the braise so the protein stays tender rather than seizing), and the structural ingredient is real Burgundy (a fruity acidic pinot, not a heavy California cab); the dish reads like the wine it was cooked in.

ingredients.item: slab bacon or pancetta, in 1 cm cubes
red Burgundy or pinot noir (full bottle, dry, medium-body)
bouquet garni (thyme, bay, parsley stems tied with twine)
pearl onions (frozen peeled, or fresh blanched and peeled)
granulated sugar (for the pearl onion glaze)

title: Beef rendang
desc: Padang dry-cooked beef. Chuck or shank simmered in spice paste and coconut milk for three to four hours until the liquid completely evaporates and the meat finishes by frying in its own released coconut oil, producing a black-brown lacquered exterior and a paste that coats every fiber. The wet stage in the middle is kalio; the dry endpoint is rendang.

ingredients.item: dried red Holland or Fresno chilies
kosher salt, additional for the braise

title: Big Mac sauce (homemade copycat)
desc: The McDonald's Big Mac special sauce, built on a real-egg mayonnaise base with sweet pickle relish, yellow mustard, white wine vinegar, paprika, garlic powder, and onion powder. Tastes substantially cleaner than the franchise version because the mayo is fresh and the relish is real chopped pickle rather than a corn-syrup gel.

title: Bigos (Polish hunter's stew)
desc: Sauerkraut and fresh cabbage cooked low and slow with multiple meats (pork shoulder, kielbasa, smoked bacon), dried porcini, dried plum, juniper, caraway, and bay, then improved by 3 to 5 days of reheating. The Polish national stew; structurally a kraut-and-meat braise that genuinely gets better with each day of rewarming, the kind of dish made on a Sunday and eaten through Thursday.

ingredients.item: pork shoulder (boneless), cut into 3 cm chunks
smoked Polish kielbasa (or any quality smoked pork sausage)
fresh green cabbage, cored and shredded
yellow onion, peeled and finely diced
allspice berries (or ¼ tsp ground allspice)
dry red wine (or beef stock as substitute)

title: Breakfast egg bites with spinach and feta
desc: Whole eggs blended briefly with cottage cheese, baked in a buttered standard metal muffin tin with wilted spinach, crumbled feta, scallion, and dill. The cottage cheese is the entire technique: the curds break apart in the blender into a smooth custard-like base, and the casein proteins contribute structural body that keeps the bites tender and moist rather than the rubbery quiche-muffin texture that home egg cups usually produce. Baked low and slow at 165 °C in a water bath setup, which prevents the eggs from puffing dramatically and then collapsing into denseness. Make-ahead breakfast, holds 5 days refrigerated.

ingredients.item: unsalted butter, cold (for the muffin tin)

title: British fish and chips
desc: The British chip-shop standard: thick-cut floury potatoes blanched at low temperature then fried hot for the shattering chip exterior, with cod or haddock fillets battered in a cold beer-and-flour batter (the cold batter plus hot oil creates the puffy lacy exterior) and fried in the same oil. Served with malt vinegar, salt, and mushy peas.

ingredients.item: ice-cold British bitter or lager (Newcastle Brown, Bass, or any pale ale)
cod or haddock fillets, skinless, 2 cm thick
malt vinegar (Sarson's is the canonical UK brand)

title: British Victoria sponge
desc: The structural English afternoon-tea cake: a 4-4-4-4 equal-weight sponge (butter, sugar, eggs, self-raising flour) baked in two 20 cm tins, sandwiched with raspberry jam and lightly whipped cream, and dusted with caster sugar. The simplest possible cake in technique and the most demanding in execution: every variable (butter temperature, egg incorporation, oven heat) directly affects the crumb.

ingredients.item: unsalted butter, soft and pliable at room temperature
raspberry jam (good quality, with seeds for the canonical version)

title: Bulgogi (Asian pear-marinated grilled beef)
desc: Paper-thin ribeye marinated in a paste of grated Asian pear, soy sauce, brown sugar, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, and scallion, the fruit enzymes tenderizing the meat while the sugars set up for Maillard browning. Seared hard in a smoking cast-iron pan or over a tabletop grill in 60 seconds per side, then served with rice, lettuce wraps, and ssamjang. The canonical Korean grilled-meat dish, the prototype for the whole Korean barbecue tradition.

ingredients.item: ribeye, sirloin, or chuck eye, sliced 1 to 2 mm thick across the grain
Asian pear, peeled and grated on a box grater
soy sauce (Korean jin-ganjang or Japanese koikuchi shoyu)
garlic cloves, grated on a microplane
fresh ginger, grated on a microplane
sake or mirin (Korean cooking traditionally uses cheongju)
cooked Japanese short-grain or Korean short-grain rice
butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce (for wraps)
ssamjang (Korean BBQ wrap sauce, store-bought or homemade)
green chilies (Korean cheongyang or Thai bird), thin-sliced

title: Buttermilk biscuits and sausage gravy
desc: Southern breakfast on a plate: tall flaky buttermilk biscuits made with cold butter and cold buttermilk (cold-fat-everything is the structural rule), split in half on the plate and smothered with sausage gravy built from rendered breakfast pork sausage, the pan drippings dusted with flour for a roux, and whisked with whole milk to a smooth peppery white gravy.

ingredients.item: all-purpose flour (or White Lily Southern soft-wheat flour), chilled
unsalted butter, cubed and frozen 15 min
breakfast pork sausage (Jimmy Dean Regular or similar)

title: Cajun andouille and chicken jambalaya (brown, no tomato)
desc: The Cajun (Acadiana) jambalaya: chicken thighs and smoked andouille sausage browned hard to build a deep mahogany fond, the holy trinity (onion, celery, bell pepper) sweated in the rendered fat, then long-grain rice toasted in the fond and cooked absorption-style in chicken stock with Cajun seasoning. No tomato. The "brown jambalaya" of the prairie Cajuns, distinct from the red Creole jambalaya of New Orleans.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (only if needed; chicken and andouille usually render enough)
Cajun seasoning (Tony Chachere's, Slap Ya Mama, or homemade from blackened-redfish blend)
Crystal or Tabasco hot sauce (at the table)

title: Cajun blackened redfish
desc: The Paul Prudhomme 1980 New Orleans technique: thick redfish fillets dipped in melted butter, dredged in a hot dry spice blend (paprika, cayenne, garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, thyme, black and white pepper), and seared in a screaming-hot dry cast iron skillet that turns the spice crust to a black, audibly crackling, intensely seasoned crust. The technique is designed to cook the fish from spice-crust to flaky in under 5 minutes.

ingredients.item: paprika (sweet Hungarian or Spanish, not smoked)
redfish, red snapper, grouper, or sea bass fillets (skinless, 2 cm thick)

title: Cajun boudin blanc
desc: The Cajun rice-and-pork sausage of Acadiana: pork shoulder and pork liver cooked tender with the holy trinity and Cajun spice, ground coarse, mixed with cooked rice, scallions, and parsley, then stuffed into natural hog casings and gently poached (never boiled). Eaten by squeezing the soft filling out of the casing directly into the mouth at the Cajun roadside boudin stand.

ingredients.item: boneless pork shoulder, in 4 cm chunks

title: Cajun crawfish étouffée
desc: The Cajun crawfish smother: holy trinity sweated in butter, dusted with a light blond roux for body, simmered with stock and crawfish fat into a smooth sauce, then crawfish tail meat folded in at the end so it warms through without overcooking. Served over white rice. The structural Cajun spring dish during crawfish season (February to May in Louisiana).

ingredients.item: Louisiana crawfish tail meat (with fat), thawed

title: Carnitas
desc: The Michoacán pork confit: bone-in shoulder slowly simmered in lard with citrus, milk, and aromatics until tender, then cranked up to crisp the surface against the rendered fat. Shredded into ragged pieces with caramelized edges and yielding centers, served on warm corn tortillas with cilantro, white onion, salsa verde, and lime.

title: Chia coconut pudding with mango
desc: Chia seeds soaked overnight in full-fat coconut milk with maple syrup, vanilla, and a pinch of salt. The chia seeds absorb roughly 12 times their weight in liquid and gel into a soft tapioca-like pudding without cooking. Topped at serving with fresh or thawed-frozen mango (cut into 1 cm dice), toasted coconut flakes, and a small squeeze of lime that brightens the coconut-mango pairing. Vegan, gluten-free, no-cook for the pudding itself. Make-ahead breakfast or dessert; the texture continues to improve through 24 hours and holds 4 days refrigerated.

ingredients.item: unsweetened almond milk or oat milk (to thin slightly)
unsweetened coconut flakes (large flakes, not desiccated)

title: Chicken adobo
desc: The Filipino national dish. Chicken thighs braised in vinegar, soy sauce, garlic, bay, and black peppercorns, then reduced to a dark, glossy glaze that lacquers the skin and concentrates the acetic-soy-fermented depth into a single dominant flavor. The vinegar is the soul, the soy is the regional addition, and the long reduction is what separates a real adobo from a wet braise.

ingredients.item: cane vinegar (sukang iloko or Filipino cane vinegar)
neutral oil for the final sear

title: Chicken and andouille gumbo
desc: Bone-in chicken thighs and sliced andouille simmered in a chocolate-brown roux base built on the Cajun Trinity (onion, celery, bell pepper), thickened over 90 minutes into a coffee-dark, deeply layered stew. The roux is the recipe, 30 to 45 minutes of attentive stirring of equal-weight flour and oil from blond through peanut to dark mahogany takes the dish from soup-with-chicken-in-it to gumbo.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (peanut, vegetable, or rendered chicken fat from Phase 1)
chicken stock, low-sodium or unsalted homemade
hot sauce (Crystal, Tabasco, or Louisiana Gold)

title: Chicken karaage (Japanese fried chicken)
desc: Boneless chicken thighs cut into 4 cm chunks, marinated in soy sauce, sake, grated ginger, and grated garlic for 30 to 60 minutes, then double-tossed in potato starch (not flour) and double-fried in 170°C oil for an audibly shatter-crisp shell around juicy bite-sized pieces. Served with a lemon wedge and Kewpie mayo. The izakaya canonical, the bento favorite, and the case study in why double-frying produces crispier crust than single-frying.

ingredients.item: boneless skin-on chicken thighs, cut into 4 cm chunks
fresh ginger, grated on a microplane
garlic cloves, grated on a microplane
neutral oil (canola, vegetable, peanut, or rice bran) for deep-frying
green cabbage, very thinly shredded (the canonical accompaniment)

title: Chicken paprikash (csirkepaprikás)
desc: Bone-in chicken legs braised in a deeply paprika-bloomed onion base, finished off-heat with full-fat sour cream tempered into the sauce for a creamy, brick-red, faintly tangy gravy that coats the chicken without breaking. Served over nokedli (small Hungarian egg dumplings) or buttered noodles; the canonical Hungarian Sunday lunch.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken legs, separated at the joint into drumsticks and thighs
lard (canonical) or rendered bacon fat, or neutral oil
yellow onion, peeled and finely diced
garlic, peeled and microplaned to a paste
green bell pepper (or Hungarian wax pepper), stemmed, seeded, diced
Hungarian hot paprika or cayenne (optional, for heat)
tomato, peeled and diced (or 1 tbsp tomato paste)
chicken stock (or water if no stock)
full-fat sour cream (at least 14% fat, ideally 18 to 20%), at room temperature
all-purpose flour (for stabilizing the sour cream)
fine sea salt (for the nokedli batter and boiling water)

title: Chicken satay with peanut sauce
desc: Indonesian-Malaysian skewers of marinated chicken thigh grilled fast over high heat for char and smoke, served with a peanut sauce built on roasted peanuts, fried bumbu, tamarind, palm sugar, and a thread of kecap manis. The marinade browns aggressively from the kecap manis sugar; the sauce balances roasted-peanut richness against acid and chili.

title: Chicken shawarma
desc: The vertical-spit kebab reformatted for the home oven: boneless chicken thighs marinated 4 to 24 hours in a deep seven-spice blend (cumin, coriander, allspice, cardamom, paprika, cinnamon, turmeric), garlic, lemon, and yogurt, then layered on a sheet pan or roasted vertically on a skewered stack and finished under the broiler for the crisp edges that define shawarma. Sliced thin and served in warm pita with tahini-yogurt sauce, sliced tomato, cucumber, red onion, parsley, and toum (Lebanese garlic sauce) or pickled turnips. The structural defining feature is the seven-spice blend; the structural cooking feature is the layered roast that approximates the vertical spit.

ingredients.item: tahini-yogurt sauce (or pure tahini sauce / tarator)

title: Chicken tagine with preserved lemon and olives
desc: The canonical Moroccan tagine: chicken thighs and legs slow-braised over a bed of sliced onion in a pool of olive oil, saffron-bloomed water, ginger, turmeric, cumin, garlic, and cilantro, with finely chopped preserved-lemon rind and meaty green olives added in the last twenty minutes so the lemon stays bright and the olives stay firm. The conical tagine lid (or a tight Dutch oven lid) is structural; the dome condenses steam and returns it to the dish, self-basting and concentrating flavor with very little added liquid. The defining Moroccan plate worldwide and the most reliable introduction to tagine technique.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks
cracked green Moroccan olives (picholine, beldi, or castelvetrano)

title: Chicken-zucchini meatballs with yogurt-dill
desc: Ground chicken thigh mixed with salt-drained grated zucchini, grated onion, garlic, lemon zest, fresh dill, and a small panade of breadcrumbs hydrated in milk. The zucchini contributes moisture that keeps the lean chicken from drying out during the bake, and the panade traps that moisture in a starch matrix that releases gradually as the meatballs cook. Baked at 220 °C on parchment-lined sheet pans (no frying, no nonstick), served with a cold lemon-dill yogurt sauce that doubles as both dressing and dip. Mediterranean leaning, weeknight feasible, the meatballs hold beautifully for next-day lunches.

ingredients.item: zucchini, grated on the large holes of a box grater
kosher salt (for the zucchini drain only)
small yellow onion, grated on the large holes
fresh dill, fronds and tender stems, finely chopped
flat-leaf parsley, leaves and tender stems, finely chopped
extra-virgin olive oil (for brushing the meatballs and the parchment)

title: Chiles rellenos (Mexican fried-batter stuffed poblanos)
desc: Roasted-and-peeled poblano chiles stuffed with melting cheese, dipped in a stiff egg-white batter, shallow-fried until puffed and golden, then bathed in a thin tomato-broth caldillo. The structural central-Mexican preparation: the egg-white batter is what defines the dish, the technique is closer to soufflé than to tempura, and the result is a soft cheese-filled chile in a crisp puffy shell sitting in a light broth.

ingredients.item: queso Oaxaca or low-moisture mozzarella, shredded
queso fresco, crumbled (for added Mexican flavor depth)
all-purpose flour (for dredging the chiles before battering)

equipment: gas burner or broiler for roasting chiles

title: Chipotle in adobo (homemade)
desc: Dried chipotle morita peppers toasted on a comal, rehydrated in just-boiled water, then blended with tomato, apple cider vinegar, onion, garlic, piloncillo, Mexican oregano, cumin, and bay, the paste fried in oil to develop Maillard depth and cooled into a thick spoonable condiment. The structural Mexican smoke-and-acid condiment: dramatically fresher than the canned commercial version, with the cook controlling the heat level and the sweetness balance.

ingredients.item: whole peeled canned tomatoes (San Marzano or similar)
avocado oil or other neutral high-heat oil

equipment: dry comal or cast-iron skillet for toasting
kettle or small saucepan for water
heavy 2 L saucepan for the fry

title: Collard greens with ham hock
desc: Long-braised collard greens in a smoky ham-hock-flavored broth: a smoked ham hock simmered alone for 90 minutes to build the pot likker (the broth that defines the dish), then collard greens stripped, washed, and torn in, braised down for 60 to 90 more minutes with onion, garlic, apple cider vinegar, and red pepper flakes. The greens emerge dark, silky, soft enough to twirl on a fork, with the pot likker reserved as the prized broth ladled over cornbread.

ingredients.item: smoked ham hock (or smoked turkey neck)
light brown sugar (optional, to balance vinegar)
Texas Pete or Crystal hot sauce (at the table)

title: Coq au vin
desc: The Burgundian chicken-in-red-wine braise: bone-in chicken thighs and drumsticks marinated in red wine and aromatics overnight, then browned hard, the marinade reduced and added back, slow-braised 50 to 60 minutes until the meat falls from the bone, and finished with sautéed lardons, pearl onions, and cremini mushrooms tucked in at service. Structurally the chicken sibling of beef bourguignon: same Burgundian wine, same garnish, same lardon-based fat structure; different protein, different timing. The overnight marinade is the structural difference from bourguignon (chicken does not need to braise as long, so the wine flavor has to penetrate before the cook).

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks (mixed)
red Burgundy or pinot noir (full bottle, dry, medium-body)
bouquet garni for the marinade (thyme, bay, parsley stems)
slab bacon or pancetta, in 1 cm cubes
granulated sugar (for the pearl onion glaze)

title: Corn tortillas from masa
desc: The structural bread of Mexican cooking, made from nixtamalized corn flour (masa harina) hydrated with water and salt, pressed thin, and griddled on a hot comal until the surface puffs from internal steam. Soft, pliable, deeply corn-flavored, and dramatically better than any supermarket tortilla.

title: Cottage pie
desc: The British beef-and-potato pie: ground beef braised down with onion, carrot, celery, garlic, tomato paste, beef stock, red wine, Worcestershire, and thyme, topped with a thick layer of butter-and-cream mashed potato, then baked until the potato crust is deep gold with crispy ridges from a fork-scored surface. Distinct from shepherds pie, which is structurally identical but uses lamb.

ingredients.item: dry red wine (or stock for non-alcoholic)
frozen green peas (added at end of braise)
kosher salt (for the mash and the potato water)
grated sharp cheddar cheese (optional, for the cheesy crust)

title: Country sourdough
desc: A simple white-flour-and-whole-wheat country loaf with 200 g active levain, 4-hour bulk ferment with 4 stretch-and-folds, overnight cold proof in the refrigerator, baked in a preheated Dutch oven at 245°C for the structural oven-spring. The accessible-yet-not-dumbed-down sourdough loaf: 72% hydration, 15% whole wheat for fermentation aid and depth, the beginner-friendly Dutch-oven steam method, and a deeply mahogany crust with an open creamy crumb that justifies the 24-hour cycle.

ingredients.item: mature sourdough starter (active, fed within last 12 hours)
rice flour or fine semolina (for dusting the banneton)

equipment: Danish dough whisk or wooden spoon
10-inch banneton (or bowl lined with kitchen towel)
5 to 7 quart Dutch oven with tight-fitting lid
lame, razor blade, or sharp paring knife for scoring

title: Couscous with seven vegetables
desc: The canonical Moroccan Friday meal: medium-grain semolina couscous hand-steamed three times over a deep saffron-and-ginger broth of lamb, chickpeas, and seven layered vegetables (carrot, turnip, zucchini, cabbage, pumpkin or butternut squash, sweet potato, and tomato), the grains fluffed and oiled between steamings until they emerge separate, light, and perfumed with the broth steam. The structural defining technique is the triple steam in the couscoussier; box-instant couscous boiled in stock is a different (and inferior) dish. The dish that gives Maghreb cuisine its name to the world.

ingredients.item: canned crushed tomatoes (or 200 g fresh, grated)
fresh cilantro and parsley, tied in a bundle

title: Creole jambalaya (red, with tomato)
desc: Long-grain rice cooked in a single pot with browned chicken thighs, andouille, shrimp, the Cajun Trinity, and tomato, the grains absorbing the stock and rendered protein fat into a unified, brick-red, paella-adjacent one-pot dish. The New Orleans Creole school of jambalaya, distinguished from the Cajun (brown, no tomato) version by the addition of tomato and a more refined seasoning profile.

ingredients.item: bone-in skinless chicken thighs, cut into 4 cm chunks
long-grain white rice (Louisiana long-grain, basmati, or Carolina Gold)
chicken stock, low-sodium or unsalted homemade
hot sauce (Crystal, Tabasco, or Louisiana Gold)
shrimp (16/20 count), peeled and deveined, tails on or off

title: Cuban black beans (frijoles negros)
desc: The structural Cuban side: dried black beans cooked tender from a cold-water soak, then finished in a sofrito of olive oil, onion, green bell pepper, garlic, cumin, oregano, bay, with a splash of dry white wine and a teaspoon of sugar to round the bitter edge of the bean skin. The cooked bean liquid is reused and reduced into the finished pot for body, color, and umami.

ingredients.item: green bell pepper, whole (for the bean cook)

title: Cuban picadillo
desc: The Cuban sweet-and-savory ground beef stew: 80/20 chuck cooked down with a green-bell-pepper sofrito, tomato sauce, dry white wine, then finished with raisins, manzanilla olives, capers, and a splash of vinegar so the dish hits a structural Cuban triad of sweet-salty-acid in every bite. Served over white rice with fried sweet plantain.

title: Cuban ropa vieja
desc: The Cuban national dish: brisket simmered until tender, shredded into rough strands (the old clothes the name describes), and finished in a sofrito of onion, bell pepper, garlic, tomato, cumin, oregano, and dry sherry. Served over white rice with sweet plantains, black beans, and a wedge of lime.

ingredients.item: beef brisket (flat, with some fat cap)
canned crushed tomatoes (or canned San Marzano, crushed by hand)

title: Cuban sandwich
desc: The Tampa-Cuban exile sandwich: split Cuban or French bread layered with sliced roast pork (canonical: leftover Cuban-mojo pernil), sweet sliced ham, Swiss, dill pickles, and a thin smear of yellow mustard, then pressed in a plancha or weighted skillet until the cheese is fully molten, the bread is mahogany and crackly, and the layered fillings have compressed into a single 2 cm dense stack.

ingredients.item: Cuban bread (or French baguette, or Italian ciabatta)
unsalted butter, softened (for the outside of the bread)
roast pork (cold sliced leftover Cuban mojo pernil)

title: Doro wat (Ethiopian spiced chicken stew)
desc: Bone-in chicken legs slow-simmered in a deeply caramelized onion base bloomed with berbere and niter kibbeh, finished with whole hard-boiled eggs scored to soak up the sauce. The dish that defines Ethiopian cooking: the 45-minute onion sweat is not optional, the berbere is the structural seasoning, the niter kibbeh is the structural fat, and the result is a thick brick-red wat eaten with injera and ayib (mild fresh cheese).

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-off chicken legs (drumsticks and thighs separated)
yellow onion, very finely diced (3 to 4 mm dice)
garlic, peeled and microplaned to a paste
fresh ginger, peeled and microplaned to a paste
dry red wine or t'ej (Ethiopian honey wine)
eggs, hard-boiled and peeled, scored with a knife (3 to 4 shallow slashes per egg)

title: Egusi soup
desc: Ground West African melon seeds simmered in red palm oil with onion, scotch bonnet, smoked fish, assorted meats, and dark leafy greens. The seeds clump into soft yellow-white pebbles in the hot palm oil and thicken the stew into the characteristic grainy-bodied soup; eaten with fufu, pounded yam, or eba, scooped by hand.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs (or beef chuck cut in chunks, or a mix)
yellow onion, peeled and roughly chopped
Maggi seasoning cube (or 1 tsp bouillon powder, or 1 tbsp soy sauce)
smoked fish (smoked catfish, herring, or mackerel), boneless
dried shrimp or crayfish powder (or 2 tbsp ground dried shrimp)
ground egusi seeds (or raw shelled pumpkin seeds ground in a spice grinder)
red palm oil (the recipe default, see substitutions)
yellow onion (the reserved half from Phase 1), finely diced
scotch bonnet peppers, stemmed and roughly chopped (seeded for milder, whole for full heat)
fresh spinach or frozen chopped spinach (bitter leaf or ugu pumpkin leaves are canonical, see substitutions)

title: Enchiladas verdes (green-tomatillo enchiladas with chicken)
desc: Corn tortillas briefly pan-fried to soft pliability, dipped in a charred-tomatillo salsa verde, filled with shredded poached chicken, rolled tight, topped with more salsa verde, crema, and crumbled queso fresco, finished under the broiler for 90 seconds. The everyday Mexican central-state enchilada, structurally distinct from the red mole-or-chili-paste enchiladas rojas, and arguably the most accessible Mexican dinner to make at home.

ingredients.item: Mexican crema (or sour cream thinned with 2 tbsp milk)

title: Fesenjan
desc: The Persian walnut-pomegranate stew: walnuts toasted and ground to a coarse meal, simmered in water until the oils release and the meal turns silken; pomegranate molasses folded in until the broth turns deep mahogany-chocolate; then chicken (or duck, the canonical northern version) braised in the sauce until the meat collapses and the walnuts thicken the stew to a sauce that coats every bite. The seven-hour walnut sweat that turns the oil-bound nut into a creamy sauce is the structural technique; the sweet-sour-savory pomegranate balance is the structural flavor.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs (and drumsticks if desired)
saffron threads, bloomed (0.2 g in 15 g hot water)

title: French onion soup gratinée
desc: The Parisian bistro soup: 1.5 kg of yellow onions deeply caramelized in butter over 60 to 75 minutes until the entire pot has reduced to dark mahogany, deglazed with dry sherry and dry white wine, simmered in real beef stock with thyme and bay, ladled into oven-safe crocks, topped with a toasted baguette slice and a heavy blanket of grated Gruyère, and broiled until the cheese has melted, bubbled, and browned to the canonical dark-spotted gratin. Structurally a study in patient Maillard browning: the slow even caramelization of the onions (no shortcut, no baking-soda cheat, no high heat) is the entire technique, and the depth of the soup is exactly proportional to the patience of the caramelization.

ingredients.item: yellow onions, halved and thinly sliced root-to-tip (5 mm thick)
dry white wine (Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Grigio, or Vermentino)

title: Fresh soy milk (homemade, from soaked soybeans)
desc: Dry yellow soybeans soaked overnight in cold water, blended with fresh water in a high-powered blender, cooked at a controlled simmer for 20 minutes to deactivate trypsin inhibitor and develop flavor, strained through cheesecloth to separate the milk from the okara pulp. The structural Cantonese-Chinese breakfast staple and the foundational starting point for fresh douhua, hot-set silken tofu, and any application where the soy milk itself is the dominant flavor.

ingredients.item: dry yellow soybeans (food-grade, organic if available)

equipment: heavy 4 to 5 quart pot (cast iron or thick stainless)
cheesecloth (2 layers) or clean unscented kitchen towel
large bowl or pot for catching the strained milk

title: Full English breakfast (components and assembly)
desc: The full English (or full fry-up): pork bangers, back bacon, fried eggs, baked beans, grilled tomato, sautéed mushrooms, hash browns or fried bread, all plated together with toast and a pot of strong tea. The recipe is less about novel technique than about parallel-track timing across 7 components so everything arrives on the plate hot at the same moment.

ingredients.item: British-style pork bangers (or fresh bratwurst-style pork sausage)
British back bacon (or thick-cut streaky bacon as substitute)
medium ripe tomato, halved across the equator
unsalted butter (for the mushrooms and tomatoes)
British baked beans (Heinz UK, the blue can)
HP brown sauce or English mustard (for the table)

title: Gado-gado (Indonesian salad with peanut sauce)
desc: A composed salad of blanched vegetables (long bean, cabbage, bean sprouts, potato, carrot), hard-boiled egg, fried tempeh and tofu, and steamed lontong rice cake, all bound at the table by a thick peanut sauce built from roasted peanuts, palm sugar, tamarind, kecap manis, lime, and red chili. The structural Indonesian everyday composed-salad dish; literally "mix-mix," referring to the diner mixing the components together with the sauce at the table.

ingredients.item: neutral oil (for frying the shallot-garlic-chili paste)
red chili (Thai bird or Fresno), seeded for less heat
palm sugar (gula jawa), grated, or dark brown sugar
tamarind concentrate (or 30 g tamarind pulp soaked in 60 ml hot water and strained)
kaffir lime leaves (or 1 tbsp lime zest)
hot water (for thinning the sauce to spoonable consistency)
firm tofu, cut into 2 cm cubes
tempeh, cut into 1 cm thick slabs
neutral oil (for frying tofu and tempeh)
long beans or green beans, cut into 5 cm pieces
napa cabbage, cut into 5 cm chunks
waxy potatoes, peeled and cut into 2 cm cubes
jasmine rice (for quick lontong substitute), or 200 g pre-made lontong
krupuk (Indonesian shrimp or rice crackers, store-bought)

title: German bratwurst with onions and mustard
desc: The Bavarian biergarten standard: fresh pork bratwurst poached gently in beer-and-onion broth to cook through without bursting the casing, then transferred to a hot iron skillet for the structural Maillard sear that crisps the casing into snap-when-bitten skin. Served on a soft pretzel roll or a slice of dark rye with sweet mustard, with deeply caramelized onions and sauerkraut on the side.

ingredients.item: yellow onion, sliced into thick rings
neutral oil (for the iron skillet sear)
soft pretzel rolls (Laugenbrötchen) or fresh hot dog buns
sweet German mustard (süßer Senf, Händlmaier brand)
good sauerkraut (German jarred, not American canned), warmed

title: German rouladen
desc: Thin slices of beef top round pounded flat, smeared with mustard, layered with bacon, dill pickle, and onion, rolled tight and tied with kitchen twine, then browned hard and braised 90 minutes in red wine and beef stock with bay and peppercorns until fork-tender. The braising liquid reduces to a glossy red-brown gravy that defines the dish.

ingredients.item: German dill pickles (Spreewald style), sliced into long spears
yellow onion, medium dice (for the braise)
dry red wine (Spätburgunder / Pinot Noir, or any medium-body red)

title: Ghormeh sabzi
desc: The Persian herb stew: enormous quantities of parsley, cilantro, fenugreek leaves, and scallion sautéed in oil until the chlorophyll deepens to near-black, then braised with cubed lamb shoulder, soaked red kidney beans, deeply browned onions, turmeric, and whole pierced dried limes for 2 to 3 hours until the lamb collapses and the broth turns viscous and dark. The herb-darkening and the dried-lime sourness are the two structural signatures; the dish is the most iconic khoresh in the Persian canon and the national dish of Iran by popular vote.

ingredients.item: fresh fenugreek leaves (or 30 g dried fenugreek leaves / kasoori methi)
fresh lemon juice (or 1 extra dried lime), for finishing

title: Goi cuon (Vietnamese fresh rolls with shrimp and pork)
desc: Translucent rice-paper rolls wrapping cold rice vermicelli, poached shrimp halved lengthwise, sliced poached pork belly, lettuce, mint, Thai basil, and chives, served with two dipping sauces: a hoisin-peanut for richness and a nuoc cham for brightness. The shrimp is laid first against the paper so it shows through the wrap as the visual signature; the roll is tight and clean, never bursting at the seam.

ingredients.item: butter lettuce or red leaf lettuce

title: Gołąbki (Polish stuffed cabbage rolls)
desc: Wilted cabbage leaves wrapped around a filling of ground pork, par-cooked rice, sautéed onion, and dill, packed tightly into a Dutch oven, blanketed with a light tomato-and-stock sauce, and slow-baked until the cabbage is silken-tender and the filling is set. The Polish cabbage roll; the Russian and Ukrainian (holubtsi) and Romanian (sarmale) versions are siblings.

ingredients.item: large green cabbage (or savoy cabbage, see notes)
long-grain white rice (jasmine or basmati works)
yellow onion, peeled and finely diced
garlic, peeled and microplaned to a paste
canned whole peeled tomatoes, crushed by hand

title: Gulyás (Hungarian goulash soup)
desc: Beef chuck slow-simmered in a deeply paprika-bloomed onion base with caraway, carrot, potato, green pepper, and tomato, finished as a thick brothy soup eaten with crusty bread. The Hungarian original is a soup (gulyás), not a stew; the heavy thick "goulash" served at American steakhouses is pörkölt, a different dish. This recipe is the soup.

ingredients.item: beef chuck (boneless), cut into 3 cm chunks
lard or rendered bacon fat (preferred), or neutral oil
yellow onion, peeled and finely diced
garlic, peeled and microplaned to a paste
caraway seed, lightly crushed in a mortar
Hungarian hot paprika or cayenne (optional, for heat)
large green Hungarian wax pepper (or green bell pepper)
beef stock (or water if no stock)
waxy potatoes (Yukon Gold), peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks
carrots, peeled and cut into 2 cm chunks

title: Horiatiki (village Greek salad)
desc: The canonical village salad of Greece: ripe tomato wedges, thick cucumber half-moons, sliced red onion, kalamata olives, green bell pepper, capers, and a single thick slab of real PDO sheep-milk feta laid whole on top, dressed with peak-quality Greek extra-virgin olive oil and dried Greek rigani (no vinegar, no lettuce, no crumbled cheese). Structurally a study in restraint: peak summer vegetables, real ingredients (real feta, real Greek oregano, real Greek olive oil), and the canonical Greek table-service form where the slab of feta sits intact on top of the salad and is broken apart with the diner's fork. The most commonly bastardized Greek dish in international cooking, and the simplest to do correctly.

ingredients.item: ripe summer tomatoes (heirloom, beefsteak, or vine-ripened, NOT winter tomatoes)
real PDO Greek feta cheese (sheep-milk or sheep-and-goat blend; in a thick rectangular slab)

title: Green goddess grain salad
desc: Cooked farro tossed cold with cucumber, scallion, and a creamy yogurt-avocado green goddess dressing that goes into the blender with a large fistful of soft herbs (parsley, basil, dill, chives) and a small punch of anchovy and lemon. The dressing is the entire dish: the herbs blend into a smooth, bright-green emulsion, the avocado contributes body without dairy heaviness, and the anchovy provides the structural glutamate that makes the salad read like a salad rather than a yogurt-coated grain pile. No-cook for the dressing, cold-assembled, batch-cookable for lunches.

ingredients.item: flat-leaf parsley, leaves and tender stems
cold water (to thin to ribbon consistency)
scallions, thinly sliced on the bias
flat-leaf parsley, roughly chopped, for serving

title: Guacamole (the classic, molcajete-prepared)
desc: Ripe Hass avocado mashed with fresh-pounded white onion, jalapeño, cilantro, and salt in a molcajete (basalt mortar), brightened with lime, served immediately. The canonical Mexican guacamole: five ingredients, two textures (chunky avocado and pounded aromatic paste), no peas, no Greek yogurt, no garlic, no cumin, no tomato. The dish lives or dies on avocado quality and on the technique of pounding the aromatics first.

ingredients.item: jalapeño peppers, seeded and finely diced
fresh cilantro, finely chopped (leaves and tender stems)

equipment: molcajete and tejolote (or a wide bowl and the back of a wooden spoon)

title: Hainanese chicken rice
desc: Singaporean-Malaysian national dish. A whole chicken poached in aromatic stock at sub-simmer temperatures for tender, gelatinous skin and silky-rare meat near the bone, served at room temperature with rice cooked in chicken fat and stock, a sharp ginger-scallion oil, a fermented chili-garlic-lime sauce, and a small bowl of the poaching broth on the side. Defined by restraint and technique; almost no spice load, all on the structure.

ingredients.item: jasmine rice, rinsed until water runs clear
scallion, white and pale green parts only, sliced 2 mm thin
fresh red Holland or Fresno chili

title: Haitian diri djon djon
desc: The black mushroom rice of northern Haiti: long-grain rice cooked in the inky steeping liquid of djon-djon mushrooms (a tiny dried black mushroom unique to Haiti), with green peas, scallion, thyme, and a whole intact Scotch bonnet. The dish reads dark grey, deeply earthy, faintly oceanic, and is the structural Haitian wedding-and-holiday rice.

ingredients.item: dried djon djon mushrooms (or dried black trumpet)
whole intact Scotch bonnet (do not pierce)

title: Haitian griot
desc: The Haitian benchmark pork dish: shoulder marinated in epis (the green seasoning paste of garlic, scallion, parsley, thyme, and scotch bonnet), citrus-braised until tender, then fried to a dark crisp finish. Served with pikliz (the fiery cabbage relish), fried plantains, and rice and beans.

ingredients.item: sour orange juice (or 50/50 lime and orange juice)
pork shoulder (Boston butt), trimmed of excess external fat

title: Haitian poul en sos
desc: Haitian chicken in tomato-pepper sauce: bone-in chicken pieces marinated in epis (the Haitian green seasoning paste of parsley, scallion, garlic, thyme, bell pepper, Scotch bonnet, lime), then dry-browned and braised in a tomato-paste-and-onion sauce until the meat is fall-tender and the sauce is glossy red-orange. The structural Haitian Sunday dinner.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs and drumsticks

title: Haitian soup joumou
desc: The Haitian Independence Day soup: kabocha or pumpkin pureed into a stock built from braised beef short rib, with cabbage, carrot, turnip, celery, leek, potato, vermicelli pasta, scallion, thyme, parsley, garlic, Scotch bonnet, and a finishing lime. The soup is rich, deeply orange, faintly sweet from the pumpkin, with the chunky cooked vegetables tucked into the puree. Served every January 1st in Haitian households worldwide.

ingredients.item: bone-in beef short rib, in 4 cm pieces
fresh epis (Haitian green seasoning, see Poul en sos recipe for build)
waxy potato (Yukon Gold), in 2 cm chunks
leek, white and light green parts, sliced

title: Harira
desc: The Moroccan iftar soup: a deeply spiced lamb-tomato base bolstered with chickpeas, brown lentils, and the canonical Moroccan thickener (tedouira, a slurry of flour-and-water that takes the soup from broth to silky-bodied without obscuring the spice work), finished with fresh cilantro and parsley and a generous squeeze of lemon. Served traditionally to break the Ramadan fast at sunset, alongside dates, chebakia, and hard-boiled eggs. The structural defining technique is the flour slurry; the structural defining flavor is the layered spice work plus the bright citrus finish.

ingredients.item: lamb shoulder or lamb stew meat, bone-in pieces
canned crushed tomatoes (or 500 g fresh ripe, grated)

title: Harvest vegetable soup with white beans
desc: A one-pot vegetable soup built on a foundation of slowly sweated leek and garlic, deepened with tomato paste fond, and brought to a brothy register with diced butternut squash, cannellini beans, and lacinato kale. The cooking water absorbs the vegetable juices into a light but savory broth. Finished with lemon juice, olive oil, and grated parmesan rind that has simmered in the pot for the entire cook. Vegan-able by omitting the parm; the bean cooking liquid (the starch and amino acids that leach out as the beans simmer) does most of the structural body work.

ingredients.item: leek, white and pale green parts only
black pepper, freshly ground, for serving

title: Hoisin sauce (homemade)
desc: Fermented soybean paste (doubanjiang or yellow miso) blended with sweet soy, garlic, Chinese five-spice, sesame oil, rice vinegar, sugar, and a touch of peanut butter for body. The structural Cantonese sweet-savory condiment: the sauce that lacquers char siu, sits on the table with Peking duck, and gets brushed on Vietnamese banh mi grilled meats. The homemade version reads dramatically fresher and more aromatic than supermarket jars, with the sweet-savory balance the cook controls.

ingredients.item: doubanjiang (Chinese fermented broad bean paste) or yellow miso
smooth natural peanut butter (no added sugar)
dark soy sauce (the sweet thick Cantonese soy)
gochugaru or cayenne (for gentle heat, optional)

title: Homemade A1-style steak sauce
desc: A from-scratch take on the dark, tangy, fruit-and-spice steak sauce that A1 has owned the category on since the 1820s. Tomato paste base reduced with raisins, orange juice, tamarind, malt and white vinegars, soy sauce, mustard, anchovy, and warm spices, then blended into a thick chunky-glossy condiment. Refrigerator-improves dramatically over two weeks as the fruit acids and spices integrate.

title: Homemade mayonnaise
desc: Egg yolk, neutral oil, Dijon mustard, lemon juice, and salt emulsified into a thick glossy sauce. The 90-second immersion-blender method (whole egg + oil + acid in a tall vessel, plunge to bottom, slow lift) is the modern home-cook revolution; the 5-minute classical hand-whisk method (yolk first, oil drop-by-drop, slow stream) is the technique-builder. Either way, fresh mayo is structurally distinct from commercial: thicker, more eggy, more aggressively lemon-mustard, with the pure neutral oil background carrying the egg flavor cleanly.

ingredients.item: large eggs (whole egg for immersion blender, yolks only for hand-whisk)
fresh lemon juice (or white wine vinegar)
neutral oil (avocado, grapeseed, or refined sunflower)

equipment: immersion blender + tall narrow vessel (immersion method)
medium bowl + damp tea towel + whisk (hand-whisk method)

title: Classic hummus
desc: The serious-cook Levantine hummus: dried chickpeas soaked overnight with baking soda, simmered with more baking soda until they are completely soft and most of the skins float off (peeled by hand for the smoothest result), then blended hot with twice as much tahini as American recipes call for, with garlic, lemon, salt, ice water, and cumin, until the dip turns silken, fluffy, and pale ivory. Served warm or at room temperature, never cold, with a generous well of olive oil, a sprinkle of sumac or paprika, a handful of whole chickpeas, and warm pita. The structural defining features: chickpeas cooked with baking soda until they collapse, a high tahini ratio (half the weight of the chickpeas), ice water for the texture, and hand-peeled chickpeas for the structural smoothness.

ingredients.item: tahini (well-stirred, smooth, single-ingredient Lebanese or Palestinian)
whole reserved cooked chickpeas, for garnish
sumac or sweet paprika, for sprinkling

title: Hungarian chicken with lecsó
desc: Bone-in chicken pieces braised on a bed of lecsó: the structural Hungarian pepper-tomato sauce of yellow Hungarian wax peppers, ripe tomatoes, onion, garlic, paprika, and a splash of bacon fat for body. The chicken renders into the lecsó during the braise; the sauce thickens around the chicken into a deeply orange-red, pepper-forward, sweet-and-savory braise. Served with white rice or fresh egg noodles.

ingredients.item: bone-in skin-on chicken thighs (and drumsticks if desired)
thick-cut bacon, diced (or 30 g lard / 45 ml neutral oil)
ripe tomatoes (Roma or beefsteak), or quality canned tomatoes
hot Hungarian paprika (or pinch of cayenne)
sugar (a pinch, to round the tomato acid)

title: Hungarian stuffed cabbage (töltött káposzta)
desc: Hungarian stuffed cabbage rolls: blanched green cabbage leaves wrapped around a filling of ground pork-and-beef, rice, onion, garlic, sweet paprika, and marjoram, then braised on a bed of sauerkraut in a tomato-paprika broth with smoked sausage and bacon tucked between the rolls. Finished with a sour cream slurry stirred into the braising liquid. The structural Hungarian Christmas Eve and Sunday-dinner dish.

ingredients.item: yellow onion, fine dice (for the filling)
smoked Hungarian kolbász (or smoked Polish kielbasa)
yellow onion, medium dice (for the braise)
all-purpose flour (mixed into sour cream for thickening)

title: Ichiban dashi (first-extraction Japanese dashi)
desc: Kombu cold-soaked in water, lifted just before boiling; the water then warmed to just under boiling, katsuobushi (dried bonito flakes) sprinkled on top, steeped 3 to 4 minutes off heat, and strained. The foundational broth of Japanese cuisine: 5 minutes of active extraction from two ingredients, no boiling, no pressing. The structural broth under miso soup, suimono, simmered dishes, and most of washoku (traditional Japanese cooking).

title: In-N-Out spread (animal-style copycat)
desc: The pink-orange spread that defines In-N-Out's animal-style burger and goes on every Double-Double by default. Mayo, ketchup, sweet pickle relish, sugar, and a touch of vinegar; distinct from Big Mac sauce in that it leans markedly sweeter, uses more relish, and skips the mustard entirely. Reads as a Thousand Island simplified to its load-bearing parts.

title: Injera (Ethiopian teff sourdough flatbread)
desc: Teff flour and water fermented for 2 to 3 days into a bubbly sour batter, then cooked on a hot smooth pan into thin pliable pancakes with a bubbly-eyed top and a smooth underside. The structural bread of Ethiopian and Eritrean cooking: plate, utensil, and absorbing layer for every wat. A 3-day project; the ferment is the work.

ingredients.item: additional water, to thin the fermented batter

title: İskender kebab
desc: The Bursa invention: sliced döner-style lamb (or beef) reformatted from the vertical spit to the home skillet, layered over bite-sized pieces of toasted pide bread, drenched in a tomato sauce thinned with broth, crowned with hot foaming brown butter poured tableside, and served with a side of thick Turkish yogurt. The structural genius of the dish is its layered architecture: the bread soaks the tomato sauce from below, the meat sits in the middle, the brown butter coats the meat from above, and the cold yogurt at the side keeps each bite balanced. The unofficial national dish of Bursa and one of the most engineered plates in Turkish cookery.

ingredients.item: lamb leg (or shoulder, or beef sirloin)
canned crushed tomatoes (or 250 g fresh, peeled and chopped)
Turkish pide bread (or Lebanese pita, or country sourdough)
whole-milk Greek yogurt (or thick plain yogurt)

title: Jamaican ackee and saltfish
desc: The national dish of Jamaica: salt cod desalted in cold-water soaks, then flaked and sauteed with sweet onion, bell pepper, scotch bonnet, scallion, thyme, and tomato, with canned ackee (the buttery yellow arils of a tropical fruit) folded in at the last minute so they hold their shape. The dish reads buttery, lightly smoky, gently sweet, with a clean salt-cod chew.

title: Jamaican beef patties
desc: The flaky turmeric-tinted pastry pocket of Jamaican street and bakery culture: a butter-and-shortening shortcrust colored deep yellow with turmeric and curry powder, wrapped around a tight filling of ground beef cooked down with onion, scallion, scotch bonnet, thyme, allspice, and breadcrumbs to bind. The pastry shatters into laminated layers; the filling stays moist without leaking.

ingredients.item: cold unsalted butter, cubed and frozen 15 min
cold lard or vegetable shortening, cubed and frozen 15 min
egg, beaten with 1 tbsp water (for egg wash)

title: Jamaican curry goat
desc: Bone-in goat shoulder marinated overnight with Jamaican curry powder, scotch bonnet, allspice, thyme, scallion, garlic, and ginger, then dry-browned (the "burning" of the curry), water-braised 2 to 3 hours until the meat slides from the bone, with potato added in the last 30 minutes to thicken the gravy. The defining dish of Jamaican wedding feasts and Sunday dinners.

ingredients.item: bone-in goat shoulder or leg, in 4 cm chunks
Jamaican curry powder (Betapac, Grace, or Walkerswood)
scotch bonnet, minced (gloves on, for canonical heat)
waxy potato (Yukon Gold), in 3 cm chunks

title: Jamaican jerk chicken
desc: The Jamaican benchmark: chicken thighs marinated overnight in a wet jerk paste of scotch bonnet, allspice, thyme, scallion, garlic, ginger, soy, lime, and brown sugar, then grilled hot and finished over indirect heat. The pimento (allspice) wood smoke of the canonical roadside Jamaican jerk pit is adapted here to a charcoal grill with allspice berries on the coals.

ingredients.item: fresh thyme branches (optional, for the coals)

title: Jamaican rice and peas
desc: The Sunday-dinner partner to jerk: long-grain rice cooked in coconut milk with red kidney beans (the "peas"), thyme, scallion, garlic, and a whole scotch bonnet floated on top for fragrance without piercing heat. The bean cooking liquid is reused as the rice liquid, deepening color and umami.

title: Jollof rice (Nigerian style)
desc: Long-grain parboiled rice cooked in a deeply reduced pepper-tomato-onion base with scotch bonnet, tomato paste, and a finishing of the prized smoky bottom-of-pot caramelization. The Nigerian version of the dish that the entire West African coast claims; parboil-and-steam method, generous chili, the obe ata base cooked down until the oil splits out.

ingredients.item: long-grain parboiled rice (Uncle Ben's style, or any "parboiled" or "easy cook" labeled rice)
red bell peppers, stemmed and seeded, roughly chopped
plum or roma tomatoes, roughly chopped
scotch bonnet peppers, stemmed (seeded for milder, whole for full heat)
red onion, peeled and roughly chopped (half goes in the blend, half saved for the cook)
red onion, diced (the reserved half from Phase 2)
neutral vegetable oil (or red palm oil for traditional, see substitutions)
garlic, peeled and microplaned to a paste
fresh ginger, peeled and microplaned to a paste
Nigerian curry powder (yellow curry blend with turmeric, coriander, cumin, fenugreek)
fresh thyme leaves (or dried thyme at half quantity)
Maggi seasoning cube (or 1 tsp bouillon powder, or 1 tbsp soy sauce)

title: Joojeh kabab
desc: The Persian saffron-yogurt chicken skewer: boneless thigh meat marinated 4 to 24 hours in grated onion, bloomed saffron, yogurt, lemon, and olive oil, then threaded on flat skewers and grilled over high heat until the surface is amber-mahogany and the interior is just set. The yogurt enzymes pre-tenderize the protein; the bloomed saffron carries the color and aroma; the onion juice deepens the savory base. The structural lighter-meat companion to the heavier koobideh and a defining dish of Iranian kabab cookery.

title: Kinilaw na tanigue
desc: Filipino vinegar-cured raw fish. Spanish mackerel diced and submerged briefly in cane vinegar with ginger, chili, red onion, and calamansi, then drained and served at the just-cured edge where the fish reads firm and opaque on the surface but still translucent at the center. Pre-dates Spanish ceviche by centuries; the vinegar is the defining cure, not the citrus, and the cure is short and aggressive, not slow and gentle.

ingredients.item: tanigue (Spanish mackerel) or sushi-grade tuna fillet, skinless
cane vinegar (sukang iloko or Filipino cane vinegar)

title: Kkakdugi
